<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`** *State the Fourier integral theorem and write the conditions for its existence.*

**Model Answer:**

> **Fourier Integral Theorem:** If $f(x)$ is piecewise continuous in every finite interval and absolutely integrable on $(-\infty, \infty)$, i.e., $\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \vert f(x) \vert dx$ converges, then at every point of continuity,
> 
> $$f(x) = \frac{1}{\pi}\int_0^{\infty}\!\left[\int_{-\infty}^{\infty}f(t)\cos\lambda(t-x)\, dt\right]d\lambda$$
> 
> **Dirichlet's Conditions:**
> 1. $f(x)$ is **piecewise smooth** in every finite interval.
> 2. $f(x)$ is **absolutely integrable** on $(-\infty, \infty)$.
> 3. $f(x)$ has at most a **finite number of discontinuities**, maxima, and minima in any bounded interval.

---

### Question 2
**`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`** *Write the Fourier cosine and sine integral formulas. For what type of functions are they applicable?*

**Model Answer:**

**Fourier Cosine Integral** (for **even** functions $f(-x) = f(x)$):
$$f(x) = \frac{2}{\pi}\int_0^{\infty}\cos\lambda x\left[\int_0^{\infty}f(t)\cos\lambda t\, dt\right]d\lambda$$

**Fourier Sine Integral** (for **odd** functions $f(-x) = -f(x)$):
$$f(x) = \frac{2}{\pi}\int_0^{\infty}\sin\lambda x\left[\int_0^{\infty}f(t)\sin\lambda t\, dt\right]d\lambda$$

The cosine integral applies to even functions (or even extensions of functions on $[0, \infty)$), while the sine integral applies to odd functions (or odd extensions).

---

## Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

> **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Each Part B question carries 14 marks and is split as **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**. You may be offered an internal choice between two full 14-mark questions.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]`**

**(a)** Derive the Fourier integral representation of a function $f(x)$ starting from the Fourier series. State clearly the conditions under which this representation is valid. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Find the **Fourier cosine integral** representation of $f(x) = e^{-x} + e^{-2x}$ for $x > 0$. Hence evaluate $\int_0^{\infty}\frac{\cos\lambda x}{1+\lambda^2}\, d\lambda$. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to (a):

**Step 1:** Consider the Fourier series of $f(x)$ on $[-L, L]$:

$$f(x) = a_0 + \sum_{n=1}^{\infty}\left(a_n\cos\frac{n\pi x}{L} + b_n\sin\frac{n\pi x}{L}\right)$$

**Step 2:** With $a_0 = \frac{1}{2L}\int_{-L}^{L}f(t)\, dt$, $a_n = \frac{1}{L}\int_{-L}^{L}f(t)\cos\frac{n\pi t}{L}\, dt$, and $b_n = \frac{1}{L}\int_{-L}^{L}f(t)\sin\frac{n\pi t}{L}\, dt$, substituting and using $\cos A\cos B + \sin A\sin B = \cos(A-B)$:

$$f(x) = \frac{1}{2L}\int_{-L}^{L}f(t)\, dt + \frac{1}{L}\sum_{n=1}^{\infty}\int_{-L}^{L}f(t)\cos\frac{n\pi}{L}(t-x)\, dt$$

**Step 3:** Let $\lambda = n\pi/L$, so $\frac{1}{L} = \frac{d\lambda}{\pi}$. Take $L \to \infty$:

- First term $\to 0$ (numerator bounded by absolute integrability).
- Limits $\to (-\infty, \infty)$.
- Sum $\to$ integral: $\sum_{n=1}^{\infty} \to \frac{1}{\pi}\int_0^{\infty} d\lambda$.

$$f(x) = \frac{1}{\pi}\int_0^{\infty}\!\left[\int_{-\infty}^{\infty}f(t)\cos\lambda(t-x)\, dt\right]d\lambda$$

**Conditions:** $f(x)$ must satisfy Dirichlet's conditions: piecewise continuity on every finite interval, absolute integrability on $(-\infty, \infty)$, and a finite number of extrema per bounded interval.

#### Model Solution to (b):

**Step 1 — Inner integral.** Using the standard result $\int_0^{\infty}e^{-at}\cos\lambda t\, dt = \frac{a}{a^2+\lambda^2}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
I(\lambda) &= \int_0^{\infty}(e^{-t} + e^{-2t})\cos\lambda t\, dt \\
&= \frac{1}{1+\lambda^2} + \frac{2}{4+\lambda^2}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply cosine integral formula.**

$$
e^{-x} + e^{-2x} = \frac{2}{\pi}\int_0^{\infty}\cos\lambda x\left[\frac{1}{1+\lambda^2} + \frac{2}{4+\lambda^2}\right]d\lambda
$$

**Step 3 — Extract $\int_0^{\infty}\frac{\cos\lambda x}{1+\lambda^2}\, d\lambda$.**

A **standard Fourier cosine integral result** is:

$$e^{-x} = \frac{2}{\pi}\int_0^{\infty}\frac{\cos\lambda x}{1+\lambda^2}\, d\lambda \quad (x > 0)$$

Therefore:

$$\boxed{\,\int_0^{\infty}\frac{\cos\lambda x}{1+\lambda^2}\, d\lambda = \frac{\pi}{2}e^{-x}, \quad x > 0\,}$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`**

**(a)** Derive the **Fourier sine integral** formula. Mention the type of function for which it is applicable. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Express $f(x) = \begin{cases} 1, & 0 < x < a \\ 0, & x > a \end{cases}$ as a **Fourier sine integral** and hence evaluate $\int_0^{\infty}\frac{\sin\lambda a \cdot \sin\lambda x}{\lambda}\, d\lambda$. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to (a):

**Derivation (concise):**

For an **odd function** $f(-x) = -f(x)$:
- The integral $\int_{-\infty}^{\infty}f(t)\cos\lambda t\, dt = 0$ (odd integrand).
- The integral $\int_{-\infty}^{\infty}f(t)\sin\lambda t\, dt = 2\int_0^{\infty}f(t)\sin\lambda t\, dt$ (even integrand).

Substituting into the general Fourier integral, the cosine term vanishes:

$$
\begin{aligned}
f(x) &= \frac{1}{\pi}\int_0^{\infty}\sin\lambda x \cdot 2\int_0^{\infty}f(t)\sin\lambda t\, dt \, d\lambda \\
&= \frac{2}{\pi}\int_0^{\infty}\sin\lambda x\left[\int_0^{\infty}f(t)\sin\lambda t\, dt\right]d\lambda
\end{aligned}
$$

This formula applies to **odd functions** on $(-\infty, \infty)$, or equivalently to functions on $[0, \infty)$ that we choose to **odd-extend**.

#### Model Solution to (b):

**Step 1 — Inner integral.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
I(\lambda) &= \int_0^{\infty}f(t)\sin\lambda t\, dt = \int_0^{a}(1)\sin\lambda t\, dt \\
&= \left[-\frac{\cos\lambda t}{\lambda}\right]_0^{a} = \frac{1-\cos\lambda a}{\lambda}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply sine integral formula.**

$$
f(x) = \frac{2}{\pi}\int_0^{\infty}\sin\lambda x \cdot \frac{1-\cos\lambda a}{\lambda}\, d\lambda
$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate the stated integral.** For $0 < x < a$ (where $f(x) = 1$):

$$1 = \frac{2}{\pi}\int_0^{\infty}\frac{\sin\lambda x - \sin\lambda x\cos\lambda a}{\lambda}\, d\lambda$$

Using the identity $\sin A\cos B = \frac{1}{2}[\sin(A+B) + \sin(A-B)]$:

$$1 = \frac{2}{\pi}\int_0^{\infty}\frac{\sin\lambda x}{\lambda}\, d\lambda - \frac{1}{\pi}\int_0^{\infty}\frac{\sin\lambda(x+a) + \sin\lambda(x-a)}{\lambda}\, d\lambda$$

Applying the Dirichlet integral $\int_0^{\infty}\frac{\sin\lambda c}{\lambda}\, d\lambda = \frac{\pi}{2}$ for $c > 0$ (and $-\frac{\pi}{2}$ for $c < 0$):

**Case $0 < x < a$:** Both $(x+a) > 0$ and $(x-a) < 0$, so the second integral gives $\frac{\pi}{2} - \frac{\pi}{2} = 0$. Therefore $1 = \frac{2}{\pi}\cdot\frac{\pi}{2} = 1$. ✓

Isolating the target integral from the full expression:

$$\boxed{\,\int_0^{\infty}\frac{\sin\lambda a \cdot \sin\lambda x}{\lambda}\, d\lambda = \frac{\pi}{2}\quad \text{for } 0 < x < a\,}$$

For $x > a$: the result is **0**. (This is the **Gibbs-like** behavior of the sine integral near a sharp edge.)

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Wrong symmetry choice (–3 to –5 marks):** Students often apply the cosine formula to an odd function (or vice versa). Always check $f(-x) = \pm f(x)$ *before* writing the integral.
> 2. **Missing factor of 2 in the inner integral (–2 marks):** When you restrict to $[0, \infty)$, the inner integral naturally becomes $2\int_0^\infty$ for the symmetric term. Do **not** redundantly include a "2" if the formula already accounts for it. The formula is $f(x) = \frac{2}{\pi}\int_0^\infty \cos\lambda x \big[\int_0^\infty f(t)\cos\lambda t\, dt\big] d\lambda$ — the outer $\frac{2}{\pi}$ is the only factor of 2 you need.
> 3. **Forgetting the domain restriction (–1 to –2 marks):** The Fourier integral representation holds only at points of **continuity**. At jump discontinuities, the formula yields the **average** $\frac{f(x^+)+f(x^-)}{2}$, not $f(x)$.
> 4. **Skipping the "conditions" question (–2 marks):** KTU frequently asks for Dirichlet's conditions. Memorize the three conditions verbatim.
> 5. **Sign error in $\cos\lambda(t-x)$:** The standard form has $t - x$, not $x - t$. A sign flip gives a different (wrong) integral.

---

## 5.X Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Fourier Cosine & Sine Integrals — High-Density Summary**

- **Fourier Integral (general):** $f(x) = \frac{1}{\pi}\int_0^{\infty}\!\left[\int_{-\infty}^{\infty}f(t)\cos\lambda(t-x)\, dt\right]d\lambda$. Holds at every **point of continuity** under Dirichlet's conditions.
- **Sine-cosine split:** $f(x) = \int_0^{\infty}[A(\lambda)\cos\lambda x + B(\lambda)\sin\lambda x]\, d\lambda$ with $A(\lambda) = \frac{1}{\pi}\int_{-\infty}^{\infty}f(t)\cos\lambda t\, dt$ and $B(\lambda) = \frac{1}{\pi}\int_{-\infty}^{\infty}f(t)\sin\lambda t\, dt$.
- **Cosine integral** (even $f$): $f(x) = \frac{2}{\pi}\int_0^{\infty}\cos\lambda x\left[\int_0^{\infty}f(t)\cos\lambda t\, dt\right]d\lambda$.
- **Sine integral** (odd $f$): $f(x) = \frac{2}{\pi}\int_0^{\infty}\sin\lambda x\left[\int_0^{\infty}f(t)\sin\lambda t\, dt\right]d\lambda$.
- **Standard result 1:** $e^{-ax} = \frac{2a}{\pi}\int_0^{\infty}\frac{\cos\lambda x}{a^2+\lambda^2}\, d\lambda$, $x > 0$, $a > 0$.
- **Standard result 2:** $e^{-ax} = \frac{2}{\pi}\int_0^{\infty}\frac{\lambda\sin\lambda x}{a^2+\lambda^2}\, d\lambda$, $x > 0$, $a > 0$.
- **Dirichlet integral:** $\int_0^{\infty}\frac{\sin\lambda a}{\lambda}\, d\lambda = \frac{\pi}{2}$ for $a > 0$, $-\frac{\pi}{2}$ for $a < 0$.
- **Complex form:** $f(x) = \frac{1}{2\pi}\int_{-\infty}^{\infty}e^{i\lambda x}\left[\int_{-\infty}^{\infty}f(t)e^{-i\lambda t}\, dt\right]d\lambda$.
- **Key transition:** $L \to \infty$ turns a *discrete* sum over $n$ into a *continuous* integral over $\lambda$.
- **Symmetry check rule:** If function is given only on $[0,\infty)$, choose **even extension for cosine**, **odd extension for sine** — this is the single most important strategic decision.
- **Domain of validity:** Representation equals $f(x)$ at continuity points; equals $\frac{f(x^+)+f(x^-)}{2}$ at jump discontinuities.
- **Necessary condition:** $f$ must be absolutely integrable on $(-\infty, \infty)$ — i.e., $\int_{-\infty}^{\infty}\vert f(x)\vert\, dx < \infty$.
- **Quick trick for KTU problems:** Functions like $e^{-ax}$, $\frac{1}{1+x^2}$, and $e^{-a^2x^2}$ are classic choices because their Fourier transforms have clean closed forms.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Fourier Cosine and Sine Transform
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** GYMAT301 - MATHEMATATICS FOR ELECTRICAL SCIENCE AND PHYSICAL SCIENCE – 3 > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Fourier Cosine and Sine Transform # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Prerequisite: The Fourier Integral Theorem Before stepping into the transforms themselves, we must anchor ourselves in the **Fourier Integral Theorem**. If a function $f(x)$ satisfies the **Dirichlet conditions** (i.e., it is piecewise smooth and absolutely integrable on $\mathbb{R}$), then under suitable conditions of convergence we can write: $$f(x) = \frac{1}{\pi} \int_0^\infty \left[ \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} f(t) \cos(\lambda(x-t)) \, dt \right] d\lambda$$ Expanding the cosine and separating the even and odd parts of the integrand, we obtain the **complex form** of the Fourier integral: $$f(x) = \frac{1}{2\pi} \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \left[ \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} f(t) e^{i\lambda(x-t)} \, dt \right] d\lambda$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024):** The Fourier Cosine and Sine Transforms arise as **specializations** of the full Fourier Transform when the underlying function is defined only on the **non-negative half-line** $[0, \infty)$ and is **even** or **odd** extended to the whole real line. ## 1.2 The Fourier Cosine Transform (FCT) — Formal Definition Let $f(x)$ be a real-valued function defined on $x \geq 0$ and satisfying the Dirichlet conditions. The **Fourier Cosine Transform** of $f(x)$, denoted by $F_c\{f(x)\}$ or simply $f_c(p)$, is defined as: $$F_c\{f(x)\} = f_c(p) = \sqrt{\frac{2}{\pi}} \int_0^{\infty} f(x) \cos(px) \, dx, \quad p \geq 0$$ The corresponding **Inverse Fourier Cosine Transform** is: $$F_c^{-1}\{f_c(p)\} = f(x) = \sqrt{\frac{2}{\pi}} \int_0^{\infty} f_c(p) \cos(px) \, dp, \quad x \geq 0$$ The constant $\sqrt{\frac{2}{\pi}}$ is the **symmetry factor** that makes the forward and inverse transforms structurally identical. > [!NOTE] > **Alternative Convention:** Many textbooks (notably the one by **Weinberger** and **Sneddon**) omit the $\sqrt{2/\pi}$ factor in the forward transform and place a $2/\pi$ in the inverse. KTU follows the **symmetric convention** shown above, which is mathematically elegant and is what the 2024 scheme examiners typically expect. ## 1.3 The Fourier Sine Transform (FST) — Formal Definition Similarly, the **Fourier Sine Transform** of $f(x)$, denoted by $F_s\{f(x)\}$ or $f_s(p)$, is defined as: $$F_s\{f(x)\} = f_s(p) = \sqrt{\frac{2}{\pi}} \int_0^{\infty} f(x) \sin(px) \, dx, \quad p \geq 0$$ The corresponding **Inverse Fourier Sine Transform** is: $$F_s^{-1}\{f_s(p)\} = f(x) = \sqrt{\frac{2}{\pi}} \int_0^{\infty} f_s(p) \sin(px) \, dp, \quad x \geq 0$$ ## 1.4 Intuitive Analogy — "The Prism in a Music Studio" Imagine a piece of music (a function of time) entering a recording studio. The studio has two special instruments: - A **Mirror-Equipped Oscilloscope** (the Cosine Transform): This instrument reflects the signal to make it symmetric about time zero (an *even* extension) and then breaks it into pure cosine components. It only "hears" the symmetric part of the sound — for instance, a drum hit played forward and a mirror copy played backward. - A **Phase-Inverting Microphone** (the Sine Transform): This device creates an *odd* mirror image of the signal and then extracts only the sine components. It captures the **antisymmetric** personality of the sound. In engineering terms, when you have a physical problem defined only for $x \geq 0$ (like a half-infinite rod, a semi-infinite cable, or the right half of a beam), you essentially have the choice of "extending" the function either as **even** or as **odd**. This single choice — even or odd — is the difference between using the Cosine and the Sine Transform. > [!TIP] > **Quick Decision Rule for KTU Students:** > - Boundary condition of the form $u_x(0, t) = 0$ (Neumann) → Use **Cosine** transform > - Boundary condition of the form $u(0, t) = 0$ (Dirichlet) → Use **Sine** transform ## 1.5 Existence Conditions For the transforms to exist, $f(x)$ must satisfy: 1. $f(x)$ is **piecewise continuous** on every finite interval $[0, a]$. 2. $f(x)$ is **absolutely integrable** on $[0, \infty)$, i.e., $\int_0^{\infty} \vert f(x) \vert \, dx < \infty$. When these hold, the integrals converge and the inverse formulas recover $f(x)$ at every point of continuity. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Mathematical Genesis — Why Two Transforms? The full Fourier transform is defined over $(-\infty, \infty)$. But many physical problems in **electrical engineering** (transmission lines), **thermal conduction** (semi-infinite fins), and **quantum mechanics** (half-space potentials) are defined on the **half-line** $[0, \infty)$. To use Fourier techniques, we extend $f(x)$ to $(-\infty, \infty)$ in one of two natural ways: | Extension Type | Definition on $(-\infty, 0)$ | Resulting Transform | |----------------|------------------------------|---------------------| | **Even Extension** | $f_e(-x) = f(x)$ | Uses **Cosine** transform | | **Odd Extension** | $f_o(-x) = -f(x)$ | Uses **Sine** transform | The cosine transform therefore captures the *energy* of the signal symmetrically, while the sine transform captures its *antisymmetric character*. ## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > The following is the **master cheat-sheet** for solving KTU Module 1 problems. Memorize the structure — most exam problems are combinations of these. ### Core Transform Definitions | Transform | Forward Formula | Inverse Formula | |-----------|-----------------|-----------------| | **Fourier Cosine** | $f_c(p) = \sqrt{\frac{2}{\pi}} \int_0^{\infty} f(x) \cos(px) \, dx$ | $f(x) = \sqrt{\frac{2}{\pi}} \int_0^{\infty} f_c(p) \cos(px) \, dp$ | | **Fourier Sine** | $f_s(p) = \sqrt{\frac{2}{\pi}} \int_0^{\infty} f(x) \sin(px) \, dx$ | $f(x) = \sqrt{\frac{2}{\pi}} \int_0^{\infty} f_s(p) \sin(px) \, dp$ | ### Properties of Fourier Cosine Transform | Property | Mathematical Statement | Engineering Intuition | |----------|------------------------|------------------------| | **Linearity** | $F_c\{af(x) + bg(x)\} = a f_c(p) + b g_c(p)$ | Superposition principle | | **Shifting** | $F_c\{f(x - a)\} = \cos(ap) f_c(p) - \sin(ap) f_s(p)$ | Delay introduces phase mixing | | **Modulation by Cosine** | $F_c\{f(x)\cos(ax)\} = \frac{1}{2}[f_c(p+a) + f_c(p-a)]$ | Sideband generation | | **Modulation by Sine** | $F_c\{f(x)\sin(ax)\} = \frac{1}{2}[f_s(p-a) - f_s(p+a)]$ | Sideband generation | | **Multiplication by $x$** | $F_c\{x f(x)\} = \dfrac{d f_c(p)}{dp}$ | Frequency differentiation | | **Second Derivative** | $F_c\{f''(x)\} = -p^2 f_c(p) - \sqrt{\frac{2}{\pi}} f'(0)$ | Heat/wave equation workhorse | | **Parseval's Identity** | $\int_0^\infty \vert f(x) \vert^2 dx = \int_0^\infty \vert f_c(p) \vert^2 dp$ | Energy conservation | ### Properties of Fourier Sine Transform | Property | Mathematical Statement | Engineering Intuition | |----------|------------------------|------------------------| | **Linearity** | $F_s\{af(x) + bg(x)\} = a f_s(p) + b g_s(p)$ | Superposition principle | | **Shifting** | $F_s\{f(x - a)\} = \cos(ap) f_s(p) + \sin(ap) f_c(p)$ | Delay in time domain | | **Modulation by Sine** | $F_s\{f(x)\sin(ax)\} = \frac{1}{2}[f_c(p-a) - f_c(p+a)]$ | Frequency shift | | **Multiplication by $x$** | $F_s\{x f(x)\} = -\dfrac{d f_s(p)}{dp}$ | Frequency differentiation | | **Second Derivative** | $F_s\{f''(x)\} = -p^2 f_s(p) + \sqrt{\frac{2}{\pi}} f(0)$ | PDE solver | | **Parseval's Identity** | $\int_0^\infty \vert f(x) \vert^2 dx = \int_0^\infty \vert f_s(p) \vert^2 dp$ | Energy conservation | ### Catalogue of Standard Transforms (Vital for KTU Problems) | $f(x)$ for $x \geq 0$ | $F_c\{f(x)\} = f_c(p)$ | $F_s\{f(x)\} = f_s(p)$ | |------------------------|---------------------------|---------------------------| | $1$ (for $0 < x < a$, else $0$) | $\sqrt{\frac{2}{\pi}} \cdot \dfrac{\sin(ap)}{p}$ | $\sqrt{\frac{2}{\pi}} \cdot \dfrac{1 - \cos(ap)}{p}$ | | $e^{-ax}, \ a > 0$ | $\sqrt{\frac{2}{\pi}} \cdot \dfrac{a}{a^2 + p^2}$ | $\sqrt{\frac{2}{\pi}} \cdot \dfrac{p}{a^2 + p^2}$ | | $x e^{-ax}$ | $\sqrt{\frac{2}{\pi}} \cdot \dfrac{a^2 - p^2}{(a^2 + p^2)^2}$ | $\sqrt{\frac{2}{\pi}} \cdot \dfrac{2ap}{(a^2 + p^2)^2}$ | | $e^{-a^2 x^2}$ | $\dfrac{1}{a} e^{-p^2 / (4a^2)}$ | Special Gaussian-sine integral | | $\dfrac{1}{x}$ | Not convergent in standard sense | $\sqrt{\frac{2}{\pi}} \cdot \dfrac{\pi}{2} \cdot \text{sgn}(p)$ | ## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility The Cosine and Sine Transforms are **indispensable tools** in: 1. **Heat Conduction in Semi-Infinite Solids**: Solving the 1D heat equation $\frac{\partial u}{\partial t} = k \frac{\partial^2 u}{\partial x^2}$ on $x \geq 0$ with boundary data. 2. **Vibration of Semi-Infinite Strings and Beams**: The wave equation $\frac{\partial^2 u}{\partial t^2} = c^2 \frac{\partial^2 u}{\partial x^2}$. 3. **Signal Processing (Filter Design)**: Even-symmetric FIR filters naturally pair with cosine transforms. 4. **Quantum Mechanics (Hydrogen-like Problems)**: Radial wave equations on $[0, \infty)$ are often solved using sine transforms. 5. **Image Processing**: Discrete Cosine Transform (DCT) — used in **JPEG compression** — is the discrete analogue of the continuous FCT. 6. **Antenna Theory**: Field distributions on a half-space aperture use cosine/sine transforms. > [!NOTE] > The Discrete Cosine Transform (DCT) is the cornerstone of **MP3 audio compression** and **JPEG image compression** — every photo you take on a smartphone is essentially decomposed via a discrete variant of the very transform you are studying right now. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of the Inverse Fourier Cosine Transform We will derive the inverse formula directly from the **Fourier Integral Theorem** using an even extension. **Starting Point:** Assume $f(x)$ is defined on $[0, \infty)$. Define an *even extension* $f_e(x)$ to the whole real line as: $$f_e(x) = \begin{cases} f(x), & x \geq 0 \\ f(-x), & x < 0 \end{cases}$$ Since $f_e(x)$ is even, $f_e(-x) = f_e(x)$, and the Fourier integral for an even function simplifies to a **single-sided cosine integral**: $$f_e(x) = \frac{2}{\pi} \int_0^\infty \left[ \int_0^\infty f_e(t) \cos(\lambda t) \, dt \right] \cos(\lambda x) \, d\lambda$$ > **Reasoning:** The general Fourier integral splits into even/odd parts. Since the integrand $f_e(t)\cos(\lambda t)$ is even in $t$, the integral over $(-\infty, \infty)$ is twice the integral over $[0, \infty)$. The factor $1/\pi$ outside thus becomes $2/\pi$. Now, recognizing that the inner integral is exactly the **unscaled** cosine transform of $f(x)$, let us define: $$g(\lambda) = \int_0^\infty f(t) \cos(\lambda t) \, dt$$ The integral becomes: $$f(x) = \frac{2}{\pi} \int_0^\infty g(\lambda) \cos(\lambda x) \, d\lambda$$ To put this in the **symmetric KTU form**, we make the change of variable $p = \lambda$ and rescale. Define the *symmetric* cosine transform as: $$f_c(p) = \sqrt{\frac{2}{\pi}} \int_0^\infty f(x) \cos(px) \, dx \quad \Rightarrow \quad g(p) = \sqrt{\frac{\pi}{2}} f_c(p)$$ Substituting back: $$f(x) = \frac{2}{\pi} \int_0^\infty \left[ \sqrt{\frac{\pi}{2}} f_c(p) \right] \cos(px) \, dp$$ $$f(x) = \frac{2}{\pi} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{\pi}{2}} \int_0^\infty f_c(p) \cos(px) \, dp$$ $$f(x) = \sqrt{\frac{2}{\pi}} \int_0^\infty f_c(p) \cos(px) \, dp$$ This confirms the inverse formula. The Sine Transform derivation follows analogously, but starting from an *odd extension* and using the sine kernel. ## 3.2 Worked Derivation: FCT of $f(x) = e^{-ax}, \ a > 0$ We compute $f_c(p) = \sqrt{\frac{2}{\pi}} \int_0^\infty e^{-ax} \cos(px) \, dx$. This requires the standard integral identity (derivable via integration by parts twice): $$\int_0^\infty e^{-ax} \cos(px) \, dx = \frac{a}{a^2 + p^2}, \quad a > 0$$ **Quick proof of the identity:** Let $I = \int_0^\infty e^{-ax} \cos(px) \, dx$. Integrate by parts: $$\begin{aligned} I &= \left[ e^{-ax} \cdot \frac{\sin(px)}{p} \right]_0^\infty - \int_0^\infty (-a) e^{-ax} \cdot \frac{\sin(px)}{p} \, dx \\ &= 0 - 0 + \frac{a}{p} \int_0^\infty e^{-ax} \sin(px) \, dx \end{aligned}$$ Now let $J = \int_0^\infty e^{-ax} \sin(px) \, dx$. Integrate by parts again: $$\begin{aligned} J &= \left[ -e^{-ax} \cdot \frac{\cos(px)}{p} \right]_0^\infty - \int_0^\infty (-a) e^{-ax} \cdot \left(-\frac{\cos(px)}{p}\right) \, dx \\ &= \left( 0 - \left(-\frac{1}{p}\right) \right) - \frac{a}{p} I \\ J &= \frac{1}{p} - \frac{a}{p} I \end{aligned}$$ Substituting back: $$I = \frac{a}{p} \left( \frac{1}{p} - \frac{a}{p} I \right) = \frac{a}{p^2} - \frac{a^2}{p^2} I$$ $$I \left( 1 + \frac{a^2}{p^2} \right) = \frac{a}{p^2} \quad \Rightarrow \quad I \cdot \frac{p^2 + a^2}{p^2} = \frac{a}{p^2}$$ $$I = \frac{a}{a^2 + p^2}$$ Therefore: $$f_c(p) = \sqrt{\frac{2}{\pi}} \cdot \frac{a}{a^2 + p^2}$$ ## 3.3 Worked Derivation: FST of $f(x) = e^{-ax}, \ a > 0$ From the same integration by parts, we have $J = \frac{1}{p} - \frac{a}{p} I$, and substituting $I = \frac{a}{a^2+p^2}$: $$J = \frac{1}{p} - \frac{a}{p} \cdot \frac{a}{a^2 + p^2} = \frac{a^2 + p^2 - a^2}{p(a^2 + p^2)} = \frac{p}{a^2 + p^2}$$ Therefore: $$f_s(p) = \sqrt{\frac{2}{\pi}} \cdot \frac{p}{a^2 + p^2}$$ > [!TIP] > **Pattern Recognition Trick:** For $f(x) = e^{-ax}$, the FCT gives a **rational function with the squared denominator** having $a^2 + p^2$, and the FST gives the **same denominator** with $p$ in the numerator. This is a recurring theme — the two transforms are *complementary* in capturing even and odd behavior. ## 3.4 Worked Derivation: Modulation Property for Cosine Transform We want to show: $$F_c\{f(x) \cos(ax)\} = \frac{1}{2}\left[ f_c(p + a) + f_c(p - a) \right]$$ **Proof:** $$\begin{aligned} F_c\{f(x)\cos(ax)\} &= \sqrt{\frac{2}{\pi}} \int_0^\infty f(x) \cos(ax) \cos(px) \, dx \end{aligned}$$ Using the product-to-sum identity: $$\cos(ax)\cos(px) = \frac{1}{2}\left[ \cos((a+p)x) + \cos((a-p)x) \right] = \frac{1}{2}\left[ \cos((p+a)x) + \cos((p-a)x) \right]$$ Substituting: $$= \sqrt{\frac{2}{\pi}} \int_0^\infty f(x) \cdot \frac{1}{2}\left[ \cos((p+a)x) + \cos((p-a)x) \right] dx$$ $$= \frac{1}{2} \sqrt{\frac{2}{\pi}} \int_0^\infty f(x) \cos((p+a)x) \, dx + \frac{1}{2} \sqrt{\frac{2}{\pi}} \int_0^\infty f(x) \cos((p-a)x) \, dx$$ By definition of the cosine transform, the first integral equals $f_c(p+a)$ and the second equals $f_c(p-a)$. Therefore: $$F_c\{f(x)\cos(ax)\} = \frac{1}{2}\left[ f_c(p+a) + f_c(p-a) \right]$$ ## 3.5 Worked Derivation: Transform of the Second Derivative (Cosine) We want to evaluate $F_c\{f''(x)\}$. **Proof:** Let $f_c(p) = \sqrt{\frac{2}{\pi}} \int_0^\infty f(x) \cos(px) \, dx$. Differentiating under the integral sign (assuming sufficient smoothness): $$\begin{aligned} f_c'(p) &= \sqrt{\frac{2}{\pi}} \int_0^\infty f(x) \cdot \frac{\partial}{\partial p}\cos(px) \, dx \\ &= \sqrt{\frac{2}{\pi}} \int_0^\infty f(x) \cdot (-x\sin(px)) \, dx \\ &= -\sqrt{\frac{2}{\pi}} \int_0^\infty x f(x) \sin(px) \, dx \end{aligned}$$ Differentiating again: $$f_c''(p) = -\sqrt{\frac{2}{\pi}} \int_0^\infty x f(x) \cdot p \cos(px) \, dx = -p \sqrt{\frac{2}{\pi}} \int_0^\infty x f(x) \cos(px) \, dx$$ This leads to the **multiplication by $x$ property** we listed. To get the second derivative property, we instead integrate the original definition **by parts twice**: $$\begin{aligned} F_c\{f''(x)\} &= \sqrt{\frac{2}{\pi}} \int_0^\infty f''(x) \cos(px) \, dx \end{aligned}$$ Integrate by parts with $u = \cos(px)$, $dv = f''(x) dx$: $$= \sqrt{\frac{2}{\pi}} \left\{ \left[ f'(x)\cos(px) \right]_0^\infty - \int_0^\infty f'(x) \cdot (-p\sin(px)) \, dx \right\}$$ Assuming $f'(x) \to 0$ as $x \to \infty$: $$= \sqrt{\frac{2}{\pi}} \left\{ -f'(0) + p \int_0^\infty f'(x) \sin(px) \, dx \right\}$$ Now integrate the second term by parts with $u = \sin(px)$, $dv = f'(x) dx$: $$= \sqrt{\frac{2}{\pi}} \left\{ -f'(0) + p \left[ f(x)\sin(px) \Big|_0^\infty - \int_0^\infty f(x) \cdot p\cos(px) \, dx \right] \right\}$$ $$= \sqrt{\frac{2}{\pi}} \left\{ -f'(0) + 0 - p^2 \int_0^\infty f(x) \cos(px) \, dx \right\}$$ $$= -\sqrt{\frac{2}{\pi}} f'(0) - p^2 \cdot \sqrt{\frac{2}{\pi}} \int_0^\infty f(x) \cos(px) \, dx$$ $$\boxed{F_c\{f''(x)\} = -p^2 f_c(p) - \sqrt{\frac{2}{\pi}} f'(0)}$$ This boxed formula is the **key workhorse** for solving boundary value problems on the half-line. ## 3.6 Analytical Example: Solving a Boundary Value Problem **Problem:** Solve $\frac{\partial^2 u}{\partial x^2} = \frac{\partial u}{\partial t}$ for $x > 0, \ t > 0$, with: - $u(0, t) = 0$ - $u(x, 0) = e^{-x}$ for $x > 0$ - $u(x, t) \to 0$ as $x \to \infty$ **Step 1:** Since $u(0, t) = 0$ (Dirichlet), we apply the **sine transform** in $x$: $$U_s(p, t) = \sqrt{\frac{2}{\pi}} \int_0^\infty u(x, t) \sin(px) \, dx$$ **Step 2:** Apply the derivative rule. Note that $u(0, t) = 0$, so the boundary term vanishes: $$F_s\left\{ \frac{\partial^2 u}{\partial x^2} \right\} = -p^2 U_s(p, t) + \sqrt{\frac{2}{\pi}} u(0, t) = -p^2 U_s(p, t)$$ **Step 3:** The PDE becomes an **ODE in $t$**: $$-p^2 U_s(p, t) = \frac{d U_s}{dt} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{d U_s}{dt} + p^2 U_s = 0$$ **Step 4:** Solve the ODE. The general solution is $U_s(p, t) = A(p) e^{-p^2 t}$. **Step 5:** Use the initial condition. At $t = 0$: $$U_s(p, 0) = \sqrt{\frac{2}{\pi}} \int_0^\infty e^{-x} \sin(px) \, dx = \sqrt{\frac{2}{\pi}} \cdot \frac{p}{1 + p^2}$$ (from our earlier derivation with $a = 1$). **Step 6:** Therefore $A(p) = \sqrt{\frac{2}{\pi}} \cdot \frac{p}{1 + p^2}$, and: $$U_s(p, t) = \sqrt{\frac{2}{\pi}} \cdot \frac{p}{1 + p^2} e^{-p^2 t}$$ **Step 7:** Apply the inverse sine transform: $$u(x, t) = \sqrt{\frac{2}{\pi}} \int_0^\infty \sqrt{\frac{2}{\pi}} \cdot \frac{p e^{-p^2 t}}{1 + p^2} \sin(px) \, dp = \frac{2}{\pi} \int_0^\infty \frac{p \sin(px) e^{-p^2 t}}{1 + p^2} \, dp$$ This is the **closed-form solution** in integral form. The integral can be evaluated using contour integration but is typically left in this form for KTU Module 1 questions. # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — The Transform Pipeline The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the **operational flow** of applying Fourier Cosine and Sine Transforms in a typical KTU boundary value problem: ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Input Function f of x
