## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### **PART A: 2-Mark Short Answer Questions**

---

**Q1.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**State the ε-δ definition of $\lim_{x \to c} f(x) = L$.**

**Model Answer:**
We say $\lim_{x \to c} f(x) = L$ if for every $\varepsilon > 0$, there exists a $\delta > 0$ such that for all $x$ satisfying $0 < \vert x - c \vert < \delta$, we have $\vert f(x) - L \vert < \varepsilon$. The condition $x \neq c$ ensures that the actual value of $f$ at $c$ is irrelevant to the limit.

---

**Q2.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Evaluate $\lim_{x \to 0} \dfrac{\sin 5x}{3x}$.**

**Model Answer:**
$$\lim_{x \to 0} \frac{\sin 5x}{3x} = \frac{1}{3} \lim_{x \to 0} \frac{\sin 5x}{x}$$

Rewrite by multiplying and dividing by 5:
$$= \frac{5}{3} \lim_{x \to 0} \frac{\sin 5x}{5x} = \frac{5}{3} \cdot 1 = \frac{5}{3}$$

$$\boxed{\frac{5}{3}}$$

---

**Q3.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`
**Define the Left-Hand Limit and Right-Hand Limit. When does a two-sided limit exist?**

**Model Answer:**
- **Left-Hand Limit:** $\lim_{x \to c^-} f(x) = L_1$ means $f(x) \to L_1$ as $x \to c$ from values less than $c$.
- **Right-Hand Limit:** $\lim_{x \to c^+} f(x) = L_2$ means $f(x) \to L_2$ as $x \to c$ from values greater than $c$.

A two-sided limit $\lim_{x \to c} f(x)$ exists **if and only if** $L_1 = L_2$.

---

**Q4.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`
**List any four indeterminate forms of limits.**

**Model Answer:**
The four indeterminate forms are:
1. $\dfrac{0}{0}$
2. $\dfrac{\infty}{\infty}$
3. $0 \cdot \infty$
4. $\infty - \infty$

(Other valid answers include: $0^0$, $1^{\infty}$, $\infty^0$.)

---

### **PART B: 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)**

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**a)** Evaluate the following limits:
  **(i)** $\lim_{x \to 3} \dfrac{x^2 - 9}{x^2 - 5x + 6}$ **(5 marks)**
  **(ii)** $\lim_{x \to \infty} \dfrac{4x^3 - 2x + 1}{2x^3 + 7x^2 - 5}$ **(2 marks)**

**b)** Evaluate $\lim_{x \to 0} \dfrac{1 - \cos 4x}{x^2}$ using the standard limit identity. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Question A:**

**a)(i)** $\lim_{x \to 3} \dfrac{x^2 - 9}{x^2 - 5x + 6}$

**Step 1:** Direct substitution: $\dfrac{9 - 9}{9 - 15 + 6} = \dfrac{0}{0}$ — indeterminate.

**Step 2:** Factor numerator using $a^2 - b^2 = (a-b)(a+b)$:
$$x^2 - 9 = (x-3)(x+3)$$

**Step 3:** Factor denominator by finding roots of $x^2 - 5x + 6 = 0$:
$$x^2 - 5x + 6 = (x-2)(x-3)$$

**Step 4:** Rewrite and cancel the common factor $(x-3)$ (valid for $x \neq 3$):
$$\lim_{x \to 3} \frac{(x-3)(x+3)}{(x-2)(x-3)} = \lim_{x \to 3} \frac{x+3}{x-2}$$

**Step 5:** Substitute $x = 3$:
$$= \frac{3 + 3}{3 - 2} = \frac{6}{1} = 6$$

$$\boxed{\lim_{x \to 3} \frac{x^2 - 9}{x^2 - 5x + 6} = 6}$$

**a)(ii)** $\lim_{x \to \infty} \dfrac{4x^3 - 2x + 1}{2x^3 + 7x^2 - 5}$

**Step 1:** Identify $\frac{\infty}{\infty}$ form.

**Step 2:** Degrees of numerator and denominator are both 3. By the leading coefficient rule:
$$\lim_{x \to \infty} = \frac{\text{leading coefficient of numerator}}{\text{leading coefficient of denominator}} = \frac{4}{2} = 2$$

$$\boxed{\lim_{x \to \infty} \frac{4x^3 - 2x + 1}{2x^3 + 7x^2 - 5} = 2}$$

**b)** $\lim_{x \to 0} \dfrac{1 - \cos 4x}{x^2}$

**Step 1:** Direct substitution: $\dfrac{1 - 1}{0} = \dfrac{0}{0}$ — indeterminate.

**Step 2:** Multiply numerator and denominator by the conjugate $(1 + \cos 4x)$:
$$\lim_{x \to 0} \frac{(1 - \cos 4x)(1 + \cos 4x)}{x^2 (1 + \cos 4x)}$$

**Step 3:** Apply difference of squares: $1 - \cos^2 4x = \sin^2 4x$:
$$\lim_{x \to 0} \frac{\sin^2 4x}{x^2 (1 + \cos 4x)}$$

**Step 4:** Rewrite the squared sine as a product of two identical factors:
$$\lim_{x \to 0} \left(\frac{\sin 4x}{x}\right)^2 \cdot \frac{1}{1 + \cos 4x}$$

**Step 5:** Convert $\dfrac{\sin 4x}{x} = 4 \cdot \dfrac{\sin 4x}{4x}$:
$$\lim_{x \to 0} \left(4 \cdot \frac{\sin 4x}{4x}\right)^2 \cdot \frac{1}{1 + \cos 4x}$$

**Step 6:** Apply standard limits:
- $\lim_{x \to 0} \dfrac{\sin 4x}{4x} = 1$
- $\lim_{x \to 0} \cos 4x = \cos 0 = 1$

**Step 7:** Substitute:
$$= (4 \cdot 1)^2 \cdot \frac{1}{1 + 1} = 16 \cdot \frac{1}{2} = 8$$

$$\boxed{\lim_{x \to 0} \frac{1 - \cos 4x}{x^2} = 8}$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**a)** Evaluate the following:
  **(i)** $\lim_{x \to 0} \dfrac{\tan 3x}{\sin 5x}$ **(4 marks)**
  **(ii)** $\lim_{x \to 0} \dfrac{\sqrt{1 + x} - 1}{x}$ **(3 marks)**

**b)** Evaluate $\lim_{x \to \infty} \left(1 + \dfrac{2}{x}\right)^{5x}$ using the standard exponential limit. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Question B:**

**a)(i)** $\lim_{x \to 0} \dfrac{\tan 3x}{\sin 5x}$

**Step 1:** Direct substitution: $\dfrac{0}{0}$ — indeterminate.

**Step 2:** Express $\tan 3x = \dfrac{\sin 3x}{\cos 3x}$:
$$\lim_{x \to 0} \frac{\sin 3x}{\cos 3x \cdot \sin 5x}$$

**Step 3:** Split into product of three limits:
$$\lim_{x \to 0} \frac{\sin 3x}{x} \cdot \frac{1}{\cos 3x} \cdot \frac{x}{\sin 5x}$$

**Step 4:** Rewrite to match the standard form:
$$\lim_{x \to 0} \left(3 \cdot \frac{\sin 3x}{3x}\right) \cdot \frac{1}{\cos 3x} \cdot \frac{1}{5 \cdot \frac{\sin 5x}{5x}}$$

**Step 5:** Apply standard limits:
- $\lim_{x \to 0} \dfrac{\sin 3x}{3x} = 1$
- $\lim_{x \to 0} \cos 3x = \cos 0 = 1$
- $\lim_{x \to 0} \dfrac{\sin 5x}{5x} = 1$

**Step 6:** Substitute:
$$= 3 \cdot 1 \cdot \frac{1}{1} \cdot \frac{1}{5 \cdot 1} = \frac{3}{5}$$

$$\boxed{\lim_{x \to 0} \frac{\tan 3x}{\sin 5x} = \frac{3}{5}}$$

**a)(ii)** $\lim_{x \to 0} \dfrac{\sqrt{1 + x} - 1}{x}$

**Step 1:** Direct substitution: $\dfrac{1 - 1}{0} = \dfrac{0}{0}$ — indeterminate.

**Step 2:** Multiply numerator and denominator by the conjugate $(\sqrt{1+x} + 1)$:
$$\lim_{x \to 0} \frac{(\sqrt{1+x} - 1)(\sqrt{1+x} + 1)}{x(\sqrt{1+x} + 1)}$$

**Step 3:** Apply difference of squares: $(\sqrt{1+x})^2 - 1^2 = (1+x) - 1 = x$:
$$\lim_{x \to 0} \frac{x}{x(\sqrt{1+x} + 1)}$$

**Step 4:** Cancel $x$ (valid for $x \neq 0$):
$$\lim_{x \to 0} \frac{1}{\sqrt{1+x} + 1}$$

**Step 5:** Substitute $x = 0$:
$$= \frac{1}{\sqrt{1+0} + 1} = \frac{1}{1 + 1} = \frac{1}{2}$$

$$\boxed{\lim_{x \to 0} \frac{\sqrt{1+x} - 1}{x} = \frac{1}{2}}$$

**b)** $\lim_{x \to \infty} \left(1 + \dfrac{2}{x}\right)^{5x}$

**Step 1:** Identify the form: base $= 1 + \dfrac{2}{x} \to 1$ and exponent $= 5x \to \infty$. This is the $1^{\infty}$ indeterminate form.

**Step 2:** Use the standard identity:
$$\lim_{x \to \infty} \left(1 + \frac{a}{x}\right)^{x} = e^{a}$$

**Step 3:** Rewrite the expression to match the standard form. Factor the exponent 5:
$$\lim_{x \to \infty} \left[\left(1 + \frac{2}{x}\right)^{x}\right]^{5}$$

**Step 4:** Apply the identity with $a = 2$:
$$\left[\lim_{x \to \infty} \left(1 + \frac{2}{x}\right)^{x}\right]^{5} = (e^2)^{5}$$

**Step 5:** Simplify:
$$= e^{2 \cdot 5} = e^{10}$$

$$\boxed{\lim_{x \to \infty} \left(1 + \frac{2}{x}\right)^{5x} = e^{10}}$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Cancellation Error:** Many students cancel terms without factoring first. For instance, in $\lim_{x \to 2} \frac{x^2-4}{x-2}$, you **MUST** factor as $\frac{(x-2)(x+2)}{x-2} = x+2$ before canceling, not just "see" $x^2-4 \approx x^2$ and substitute.
> 2. **Forgetting the Constant Multiplier:** For $\lim_{x \to 0} \frac{\sin 5x}{x}$, students often write $= 1$ directly. The correct form is $\lim_{x \to 0} 5 \cdot \frac{\sin 5x}{5x} = 5 \cdot 1 = 5$. **Marks are deducted for this.**
> 3. **Conjugate Misuse:** When rationalizing, you must multiply BOTH numerator and denominator by the conjugate. Multiplying only the numerator changes the value.
> 4. **$1^\infty \neq 1$:** Students often incorrectly conclude that $(1 + \frac{1}{n})^n = 1$ because the base "looks like" 1. It actually approaches $e$. This is a very common 2-mark deduction.
> 5. **Radian Assumption:** The standard limits $\frac{\sin x}{x} = 1$ assume $x$ is in **radians**, not degrees. In KTU papers, $x$ is always in radians unless specified.
> 6. **Degree Comparison Rule:** Only valid for $\frac{\infty}{\infty}$ form. For $\frac{0}{0}$ rational functions, **always** factor first.

---

### **Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember**

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Module 2 – Limits**

- ✅ **ε-δ definition** is the rigorous foundation; understand but use intuitive methods for exams.
- ✅ **Limit exists** if and only if **left-hand = right-hand** limit (must be equal, both finite).
- ✅ **Direct substitution** is always Step 1. If you get $\frac{0}{0}$ or $\frac{\infty}{\infty}$, proceed with algebraic methods.
- ✅ **Standard Limits to Memorize:**
  - $\lim_{x \to 0} \frac{\sin x}{x} = 1$ and $\lim_{x \to 0} \frac{\tan x}{x} = 1$
  - $\lim_{x \to 0} \frac{1 - \cos x}{x^2} = \frac{1}{2}$
  - $\lim_{x \to 0} (1 + x)^{1/x} = e$ and $\lim_{x \to \infty} (1 + \frac{a}{x})^x = e^a$
  - $\lim_{x \to 0} \frac{\ln(1+x)}{x} = 1$ and $\lim_{x \to 0} \frac{e^x - 1}{x} = 1$
- ✅ **Techniques Priority Order:** Direct Substitution → Factoring → Rationalization → Standard Limits → L'Hôpital's Rule.
- ✅ **Rational Function at Infinity:** Compare degrees of numerator and denominator. If equal, ratio of leading coefficients gives the answer.
- ✅ **L'Hôpital's Rule** applies only to $\frac{0}{0}$ and $\frac{\infty}{\infty}$ forms — convert other forms into these.
- ✅ **Indeterminate ≠ Undefined:** Forms like $\frac{0}{0}$ are indeterminate (need evaluation), not undefined like $\frac{1}{0}$ (no limit).
- ✅ **Constant Coefficient Trick:** $\frac{\sin(ax)}{x} = a \cdot \frac{\sin(ax)}{ax}$. Don't forget the constant factor $a$.
- ✅ **Limit ≠ Function Value:** $f(c)$ may differ from $\lim_{x \to c} f(x)$ or may not even exist. Limits describe *approach behavior*.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Continuity
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** GYMAT301 - MATHEMATICS FOR ELECTRICAL SCIENCE AND PHYSICAL SCIENCE – 3
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Continuity

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of Continuity at a Point

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (Rigorously Tested):**
> A function $f(x)$ is said to be **continuous at a point** $x = a$ if the following **three conditions** are simultaneously satisfied:
>
> **Condition 1 (Existence of Function Value):** $f(a)$ is defined (i.e., $a$ lies in the domain of $f$).
>
> **Condition 2 (Existence of Limit):** $\displaystyle\lim_{x \to a} f(x)$ exists (finite).
>
> **Condition 3 (Limit Equals Function Value):** $\displaystyle\lim_{x \to a} f(x) = f(a)$.

Symbolically, we combine these into a single elegant statement:

$$f \text{ is continuous at } x = a \iff \lim_{x \to a} f(x) = f(a)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Exam Trap:** Many students write only "$\lim_{x \to a} f(x) = f(a)$" and skip the verification of the three sub-conditions. In **3-mark questions**, always enumerate all three conditions explicitly to secure full marks.

---

## 1.2 One-Sided Continuity (Left-Hand & Right-Hand)

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition — Left-Hand Continuity at $x = a$:**
> $f$ is **left-continuous** at $x = a$ if $\displaystyle\lim_{x \to a^-} f(x) = f(a)$.
>
> **Definition — Right-Hand Continuity at $x = a$:**
> $f$ is **right-continuous** at $x = a$ if $\displaystyle\lim_{x \to a^+} f(x) = f(a)$.

**Fundamental Theorem of One-Sided Continuity:**
$$f \text{ is continuous at } x = a \iff f \text{ is both left-continuous AND right-continuous at } x = a.$$

Mathematically, this translates to the necessary and sufficient condition:

$$\lim_{x \to a^-} f(x) = \lim_{x \to a^+} f(x) = f(a)$$

---

## 1.3 Continuity on an Interval

> [!NOTE]
> **Continuity on an Open Interval $(a, b)$:**
> $f$ is continuous on $(a, b)$ if it is continuous at **every point** $x \in (a, b)$.
>
> **Continuity on a Closed Interval $[a, b]$:**
> $f$ is continuous on $[a, b]$ if:
> 1. $f$ is continuous at every point of the open interval $(a, b)$.
> 2. $f$ is **right-continuous** at $x = a$ (i.e., $\lim_{x \to a^+} f(x) = f(a)$).
> 3. $f$ is **left-continuous** at $x = b$ (i.e., $\lim_{x \to b^-} f(x) = f(b)$).

---

## 1.4 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Understanding

> [!TIP]
> **The "Pen-Lifting" Analogy:**
> Imagine you are drawing the graph of $y = f(x)$ on a sheet of paper using a pen.
> - **Continuous function** = You can draw the entire curve from start to finish **without lifting your pen** off the paper. The curve has **no breaks, no jumps, and no holes**.
> - **Discontinuous function** = At some point, you are **forced to lift your pen** (jump discontinuity), **jump to infinity** (infinite discontinuity), or **oscillate wildly** (oscillatory discontinuity).
>
> **Engineering Interpretation:** Continuous signals (voltage, current, displacement) can be processed without information loss. Discontinuities model **switching events**, **circuit breaks**, or **shock loading** in mechanical systems.

---

## 1.5 The Epsilon–Delta ($\varepsilon$–$\delta$) Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Analytical Definition (Used in Advanced Proofs & Competitive Exams):**
> $f$ is continuous at $x = a$ if, for every $\varepsilon > 0$, there exists a $\delta > 0$ such that:
> $$\vert x - a \vert < \delta \implies \vert f(x) - f(a) \vert < \varepsilon$$
>
> **Physical Meaning:** We can make $f(x)$ as close to $f(a)$ as we wish (within $\varepsilon$) by choosing $x$ sufficiently close to $a$ (within $\delta$). This captures the idea of **no sudden jumps**.

---

## 1.6 Real-World & Engineering Examples

| Scenario | Function Behavior | Continuous? |
|---|---|---|
| Temperature of a heated rod over distance | $T(x) = 5x + 20$ | **Yes** (everywhere) |
| Voltage across a charging capacitor | $V(t) = V_0(1 - e^{-t/RC})$ | **Yes** (for $t \ge 0$) |
| Step input in a digital circuit | $u(t)$ (unit step) | **No** (jump at $t = 0$) |
| Height of a bouncing ball (idealized) | Piecewise with jumps | **No** (jump at each bounce) |
| Charge on a discharging capacitor | $Q(t) = Q_0 e^{-t/RC}$ | **Yes** (for $t \ge 0$) |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Logical Steps for Testing Continuity at $x = a$

To systematically test continuity, follow this **KTU Board-approved procedure**:

1. **Step A — Domain Check:** Verify that $a$ is in the domain of $f$, i.e., $f(a)$ exists.
2. **Step B — Limit Check:** Compute $\displaystyle\lim_{x \to a} f(x)$. If it exists and is finite, proceed; else $f$ is **discontinuous** at $a$.
3. **Step C — Equality Check:** Verify $\displaystyle\lim_{x \to a} f(x) = f(a)$. If true, $f$ is continuous; if not, $f$ is discontinuous (removable type).

---

## 2.2 Classification of Discontinuities (High-Yield for KTU)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Types of Discontinuities at $x = a$:**
>
> **Type 1 — Removable Discontinuity:**
> $\lim_{x \to a} f(x)$ exists but $\neq f(a)$, OR $f(a)$ is undefined.
> *Example:* $f(x) = \frac{x^2 - 4}{x - 2}$ at $x = 2$.
>
> **Type 2 — Jump Discontinuity:**
> Both one-sided limits exist but $\lim_{x \to a^-} f(x) \neq \lim_{x \to a^+} f(x)$.
> *Example:* $f(x) = \text{sgn}(x)$ at $x = 0$.
>
> **Type 3 — Infinite (Essential) Discontinuity:**
> At least one one-sided limit is $\pm \infty$.
> *Example:* $f(x) = \frac{1}{x}$ at $x = 0$.
>
> **Type 4 — Oscillatory (Essential) Discontinuity:**
> The limit does not exist due to infinite oscillation.
> *Example:* $f(x) = \sin(1/x)$ at $x = 0$.

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Mathematical Statement | Engineering Interpretation |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Continuity at a point | $\lim\limits_{x \to a} f(x) = f(a)$ | Smooth signal behavior |
| 2 | Left-continuity | $\lim\limits_{x \to a^-} f(x) = f(a)$ | Approach from below |
| 3 | Right-continuity | $\lim\limits_{x \to a^+} f(x) = f(a)$ | Approach from above |
| 4 | Epsilon–Delta criterion | $\vert x - a \vert < \delta \Rightarrow \vert f(x) - f(a) \vert < \varepsilon$ | Rigorous smoothness test |
| 5 | Sum rule | $f, g$ cont. $\Rightarrow f \pm g$ cont. | Superposition principle holds |
| 6 | Product rule | $f, g$ cont. $\Rightarrow fg$ cont. | Multiplicative signal processing |
| 7 | Quotient rule | $f, g$ cont., $g(a) \neq 0 \Rightarrow f/g$ cont. | Impedance, transfer functions |
| 8 | Composition rule | $f$ cont. at $a$, $g$ cont. at $f(a) \Rightarrow g \circ f$ cont. at $a$ | Cascaded systems |
| 9 | Intermediate Value Theorem (IVT) | $f$ cont. on $[a,b]$, $k$ between $f(a), f(b) \Rightarrow \exists c \in (a,b)$ with $f(c) = k$ | Existence of solution |
| 10 | Extreme Value Theorem (EVT) | $f$ cont. on $[a,b] \Rightarrow f$ attains max & min | Bounded output in control systems |

---

## 2.4 Algebra of Continuous Functions

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Theorem (Sum, Product, Quotient Composition):**
> Let $f$ and $g$ be real-valued functions continuous at $x = a$. Then the following functions are also continuous at $x = a$:
>
> 1. $h(x) = f(x) \pm g(x)$
> 2. $h(x) = f(x) \cdot g(x)$
> 3. $h(x) = k \cdot f(x)$ for any constant $k$
> 4. $h(x) = f(x) / g(x)$, provided $g(a) \neq 0$
> 5. $h(x) = g(f(x))$, provided $g$ is continuous at $f(a)$

**Engineering Utility:** This theorem is the foundation of **analog signal processing**. When you cascade two continuous-time filters (e.g., an RC low-pass followed by an op-amp amplifier), the overall transfer function remains continuous in the pass-band.

---

## 2.5 Standard Continuous Functions (Universally Tested)

> [!NOTE]
> The following functions are **continuous everywhere** on their natural domains:
> - Polynomial functions: $P(x) = a_n x^n + \cdots + a_1 x + a_0$
> - Rational functions: $\frac{P(x)}{Q(x)}$ where $Q(x) \neq 0$
> - Trigonometric functions: $\sin x$, $\cos x$
> - Exponential functions: $e^x$, $a^x$
> - Logarithmic functions: $\ln x$ (on $x > 0$)

---

## 2.6 The Two Pivotal Theorems on Closed Intervals

### Intermediate Value Theorem (IVT)
> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Statement:** If $f$ is continuous on $[a, b]$ and $k$ is any real number between $f(a)$ and $f(b)$ (inclusive), then there exists at least one $c \in [a, b]$ such that $f(c) = k$.
>
> **Engineering Application:** Proving the existence of a root of a non-linear equation (used in circuit analysis, structural mechanics).

### Extreme Value Theorem (EVT)
> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Statement:** If $f$ is continuous on a **closed and bounded interval** $[a, b]$, then $f$ attains a **maximum** $M$ and a **minimum** $m$ on $[a, b]$. Moreover:
> $$m \le f(x) \le M \quad \forall x \in [a, b]$$
>
> **Engineering Application:** Guarantees bounded output of feedback control systems on finite time horizons.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Checking Continuity at a Point

**Problem:** Test the continuity of $f(x) = \dfrac{x^2 - 9}{x - 3}$ at $x = 3$.

### Step 1 — Domain Check

The function $f(x)$ is defined for all $x$ such that $x - 3 \neq 0$, i.e., $x \neq 3$.

At $x = 3$, the denominator becomes zero. Therefore, $f(3)$ is **not defined**.

$$f(3) = \frac{9 - 9}{3 - 3} = \frac{0}{0} \quad (\text{undefined})$$

### Step 2 — Limit Check

Compute the limit using algebraic simplification:

$$\lim_{x \to 3} \frac{x^2 - 9}{x - 3}$$

Factor the numerator as a difference of squares:

$$x^2 - 9 = (x - 3)(x + 3)$$

Substitute:

$$\lim_{x \to 3} \frac{(x - 3)(x + 3)}{x - 3}$$

Cancel the common factor $(x - 3)$ (valid since $x \neq 3$ near the limit):

$$\lim_{x \to 3} (x + 3)$$

Evaluate:

$$\lim_{x \to 3} (x + 3) = 3 + 3 = 6$$

### Step 3 — Equality Check

We have:
- $f(3)$ — **undefined**
- $\lim_{x \to 3} f(x) = 6$ — **exists**

Since **Condition 1 fails**, $f$ is **discontinuous at $x = 3$**.

### Conclusion

> [!NOTE]
> **Type of Discontinuity:** This is a **Removable Discontinuity** because the limit exists (equals 6) but $f(3)$ is undefined. The "hole" can be "filled" by redefining $f(3) = 6$.

**Graphical Insight:**

```
      y |
        |          /
      6 + - - - - O        ←  (3, 6) is the "filled" point
        |        /
        |      /
        |    /
        |  /
        |/_______________ x
        0       3
```

The curve $y = x + 3$ has a **hole** at $(3, 6)$.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Piecewise Function Continuity

**Problem:** Find the value of $k$ that makes $f(x)$ continuous at $x = 2$:

$$f(x) = \begin{cases} x^2 + 1, & x \le 2 \\ kx + 3, & x > 2 \end{cases}$$

### Step 1 — Function Value at $x = 2$

Since $2 \le 2$, we use the first branch:

$$f(2) = (2)^2 + 1 = 5$$

### Step 2 — Left-Hand Limit

$$\lim_{x \to 2^-} f(x) = \lim_{x \to 2^-} (x^2 + 1) = 4 + 1 = 5$$

### Step 3 — Right-Hand Limit

$$\lim_{x \to 2^+} f(x) = \lim_{x \to 2^+} (kx + 3) = 2k + 3$$

### Step 4 — Apply Continuity Condition

For continuity at $x = 2$:

$$\lim_{x \to 2^-} f(x) = \lim_{x \to 2^+} f(x) = f(2)$$

$$5 = 2k + 3 = 5$$

From $2k + 3 = 5$:

$$2k = 2 \implies k = 1$$

### Conclusion

> [!NOTE]
> The value $k = 1$ makes $f$ continuous at $x = 2$. The function then becomes $f(x) = x^2 + 1$ for $x \le 2$ and $f(x) = x + 3$ for $x > 2$.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Epsilon–Delta Proof of Continuity

**Problem:** Prove that $f(x) = 3x - 5$ is continuous at $x = 2$.

### Step 1 — State the Goal

We must show: For every $\varepsilon > 0$, there exists $\delta > 0$ such that

$$\vert x - 2 \vert < \delta \implies \vert f(x) - f(2) \vert < \varepsilon$$

### Step 2 — Compute $f(2)$

$$f(2) = 3(2) - 5 = 1$$

### Step 3 — Bound $|f(x) - f(2)|$

$$\vert f(x) - f(2) \vert = \vert (3x - 5) - 1 \vert = \vert 3x - 6 \vert = 3 \vert x - 2 \vert$$

### Step 4 — Choose Delta

We require $3 \vert x - 2 \vert < \varepsilon$, i.e.,

$$\vert x - 2 \vert < \frac{\varepsilon}{3}$$

Therefore, **choose** $\delta = \dfrac{\varepsilon}{3}$.

### Step 5 — Conclude

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Given** $\varepsilon > 0$, **let** $\delta = \dfrac{\varepsilon}{3}$. Then whenever $\vert x - 2 \vert < \delta$:
> $$\vert f(x) - f(2) \vert = 3 \vert x - 2 \vert < 3\delta = 3 \cdot \frac{\varepsilon}{3} = \varepsilon$$
>
> Hence, by the $\varepsilon$–$\delta$ definition, $f(x) = 3x - 5$ is continuous at $x = 2$. $\blacksquare$

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Continuity Quick-Decision Table

| $f(a)$ Defined? | $\lim\limits_{x\to a} f(x)$ Exists? | $\lim = f(a)$? | Conclusion |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---|
| No | Yes | — | **Discontinuous** (Removable) |
| Yes | No | — | **Discontinuous** (Jump / Infinite / Oscillatory) |
| Yes | Yes | No | **Discontinuous** (Removable) |
| Yes | Yes | Yes | **Continuous at $x = a$** ✓ |

---

## 3.5 Application of IVT — Bisection-Style Root Existence

**Problem:** Show that $x^3 - x - 1 = 0$ has a real root between $1$ and $2$.

### Step 1 — Define the Function

$$f(x) = x^3 - x - 1$$

This is a **polynomial**, hence **continuous** on $[1, 2]$.

### Step 2 — Evaluate at Endpoints

$$f(1) = (1)^3 - (1) - 1 = 1 - 1 - 1 = -1$$

$$f(2) = (2)^3 - (2) - 1 = 8 - 2 - 1 = 5$$

### Step 3 — Apply IVT

Since $f$ is continuous on $[1, 2]$, and $f(1) = -1 < 0$ while $f(2) = 5 > 0$, by the **Intermediate Value Theorem**, there exists at least one $c \in (1, 2)$ such that:

$$f(c) = 0 \implies c^3 - c - 1 = 0$$

### Conclusion

> [!NOTE]
> The equation $x^3 - x - 1 = 0$ has **at least one real root** in the interval $(1, 2)$. The actual root is approximately $c \approx 1.3247$.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Continuity Decision Flowchart

This diagram maps the logical decision tree used in **every KTU continuity problem**.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[START: Test continuity at x = a] --> B{Is f of a defined?}
    B -- NO --> C[DISCONTINUOUS: Removable type]
    B -- YES --> D{Does lim of f of x as x tends to a exist?}
    D -- NO --> E[DISCONTINUOUS: Jump or Infinite or Oscillatory]
    D -- YES --> F{Does lim of f of x as x tends to a equal f of a?}
    F -- NO --> G[DISCONTINUOUS: Removable type]
    F -- YES --> H[CONTINUOUS at x = a]
    C --> I[END]
    E --> I
    G --> I
    H --> I
```

---

## 4.2 Taxonomy of Discontinuities (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Discontinuity at x = a] --> B{One sided limits both finite?}
    B -- YES --> C{Left limit equals Right limit?}
    B -- NO --> D[Essential Discontinuity]
    C -- YES --> E[Removable Discontinuity]
    C -- NO --> F[Jump Discontinuity]
    D --> G[Infinite Discontinuity: At least one limit equals infinity]
    D --> H[Oscillatory Discontinuity: Limit does not exist due to oscillation]
    E --> I[Example: f of x equals x squared minus 4 divided by x minus 2 at x equals 2]
    F --> J[Example: Unit step function at x equals 0]
    G --> K[Example: f of x equals 1 by x at x equals 0]
    H --> L[Example: f of x equals sin of 1 by x at x equals 0]
```

---

## 4.3 Function Hierarchy: Building Continuous Functions

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Constant Function] --> D[Continuous Everywhere]
    B[Power Function x to the n] --> D
    C[Exponential and Logarithmic] --> D
    D --> E[Sum and Difference]
    D --> F[Product]
    D --> G[Quotient where denominator not zero]
    D --> H[Composition]
    E --> I[New Continuous Function]
    F --> I
    G --> I
    H --> I
```

---

## 4.4 Signal Processing Analogy (Engineering Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Continuous Input Signal f of x] --> B[Continuous Filter or Operator]
    B --> C[Continuous Output Signal g of x]
    A -. Condition .-> D[f and g continuous at point a]
    D -. Ensures .-> E[No Information Loss: Signal Integrity Preserved]
```

**Interpretation:** In analog signal processing (e.g., audio amplifiers, low-pass filters), continuity of the transfer function guarantees **smooth, undistorted output**. A discontinuity (e.g., clipping in an amplifier) introduces **harmonic distortion** and **information loss**.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Part A — Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: Define continuity of a function at a point. State the three conditions for a function to be continuous at $x = a$.**

**Model Answer:**

> A function $f(x)$ is said to be continuous at a point $x = a$ if the value of the function at that point approaches the same value as $x$ approaches $a$.
>
> **The three necessary and sufficient conditions are:**
>
> 1. **Existence of function value:** $f(a)$ must be defined.
> 2. **Existence of limit:** $\displaystyle\lim_{x \to a} f(x)$ must exist (finite).
> 3. **Equality:** $\displaystyle\lim_{x \to a} f(x) = f(a)$.
>
> When all three hold simultaneously, we say $f$ is continuous at $x = a$.
>
> **[3 marks: 1 mark for definition + 2 marks for three conditions]**

---

### Part A — Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: What is a removable discontinuity? Give an example.**

**Model Answer:**

> A **removable discontinuity** at $x = a$ occurs when the limit $\displaystyle\lim_{x \to a} f(x)$ exists and is finite, but either $f(a)$ is undefined or $f(a) \neq \lim_{x \to a} f(x)$. The discontinuity can be "removed" by redefining $f(a)$ to equal the limit.
>
> **Example:** Consider $f(x) = \dfrac{x^2 - 4}{x - 2}$ at $x = 2$.
>
> Here, $f(2)$ is undefined, but $\displaystyle\lim_{x \to 2} \frac{x^2 - 4}{x - 2} = \lim_{x \to 2} (x + 2) = 4$.
>
> By redefining $f(2) = 4$, the discontinuity is removed.
>
> **[3 marks: 2 marks for definition + 1 mark for example]**

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Always state the three conditions explicitly** before testing them. Students who directly compute the limit without mentioning the conditions lose 1–2 marks.
> 2. **Do NOT confuse "limit exists" with "function is continuous."** Existence of limit alone is insufficient.
> 3. **For piecewise functions**, check **both** left-hand and right-hand limits separately. Skipping one side costs full marks in sub-question (a).
> 4. **Show all algebraic steps** in simplification. The $\frac{0}{0}$ form must be explicitly identified before applying L'Hôpital or factoring.
> 5. **IVT questions require explicit mention** of continuity and sign change. Mere computation is insufficient.

---

### Part B — Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Modified]`

**(a)** Examine the continuity of $f(x) = \dfrac{x^3 - 8}{x^2 - 4}$ at $x = 2$. Classify the discontinuity if any. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** State and prove the Intermediate Value Theorem. Using IVT, prove that the equation $x^5 - 3x - 1 = 0$ has a real root between $1$ and $2$. **[7 marks]**

---

### Model Solution — Question A

#### Part (a) Solution

**Step 1 — Domain Check at $x = 2$:**

$$f(2) = \frac{(2)^3 - 8}{(2)^2 - 4} = \frac{8 - 8}{4 - 4} = \frac{0}{0}$$

The function is **undefined** at $x = 2$ since the denominator equals zero.

**Step 2 — Compute the Limit:**

$$\lim_{x \to 2} \frac{x^3 - 8}{x^2 - 4}$$

Factor both numerator and denominator:

- Numerator: $x^3 - 8 = (x - 2)(x^2 + 2x + 4)$
- Denominator: $x^2 - 4 = (x - 2)(x + 2)$

Substitute:

$$\lim_{x \to 2} \frac{(x - 2)(x^2 + 2x + 4)}{(x - 2)(x + 2)}$$

Cancel the common factor $(x - 2)$ (valid since $x \neq 2$):

$$\lim_{x \to 2} \frac{x^2 + 2x + 4}{x + 2}$$

Evaluate:

$$\frac{(2)^2 + 2(2) + 4}{2 + 2} = \frac{4 + 4 + 4}{4} = \frac{12}{4} = 3$$

**Step 3 — Continuity Verdict:**

- $f(2)$ — undefined
- $\lim_{x \to 2} f(x) = 3$ — exists and is finite

Since $f(2)$ is undefined while the limit exists, $f$ is **discontinuous at $x = 2$**. This is a **removable discontinuity**, removable by redefining $f(2) = 3$.

**[7 marks: 2 marks each for Steps 1–3 + 1 mark for classification]**

---

#### Part (b) Solution

**Statement of IVT:**

> If $f$ is continuous on a closed interval $[a, b]$, and $k$ is any number between $f(a)$ and $f(b)$ (i.e., $f(a) \le k \le f(b)$ or $f(b) \le k \le f(a)$), then there exists at least one $c \in [a, b]$ such that $f(c) = k$.

**Proof Idea (Standard KTU Outline):**

*Case 1:* Assume $f(a) < f(b)$. Define $k$ such that $f(a) < k < f(b)$. Let $S = \{x \in [a, b] : f(x) \le k\}$. Then $S$ is non-empty (since $a \in S$) and bounded above by $b$. Let $c = \sup S$. By continuity of $f$ and the properties of supremum, $f(c) = k$. A symmetric argument works for $f(b) < f(a)$.

**Application to $x^5 - 3x - 1 = 0$:**

Let $f(x) = x^5 - 3x - 1$. Since $f$ is a polynomial, it is **continuous everywhere**, in particular on $[1, 2]$.

Compute:

$$f(1) = 1^5 - 3(1) - 1 = 1 - 3 - 1 = -3$$

$$f(2) = 2^5 - 3(2) - 1 = 32 - 6 - 1 = 25$$

Since $f(1) = -3 < 0$ and $f(2) = 25 > 0$, we have $0$ between $f(1)$ and $f(2)$. By **IVT**, there exists $c \in (1, 2)$ such that $f(c) = 0$, i.e.,

$$c^5 - 3c - 1 = 0$$

Hence, $x^5 - 3x - 1 = 0$ has at least one real root between $1$ and $2$. $\blacksquare$

**[7 marks: 2 marks for statement + 2 marks for proof sketch + 3 marks for application]**

---

### Part B — Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Discuss the different types of discontinuities with suitable examples. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** If $f$ and $g$ are continuous at $x = a$, prove that $f + g$, $f \cdot g$, and $\dfrac{f}{g}$ (with $g(a) \neq 0$) are also continuous at $x = a$. **[7 marks]**

---

### Model Solution — Question B

#### Part (a) Solution

The function $f(x)$ is said to be discontinuous at $x = a$ if at least one of the three continuity conditions fails. There are **four major types**:

**1. Removable Discontinuity:**
The limit $\lim_{x \to a} f(x)$ exists, but either $f(a)$ is undefined or $f(a) \neq \lim_{x \to a} f(x)$.

*Example:* $f(x) = \dfrac{x^2 - 1}{x - 1}$ at $x = 1$.

$$\lim_{x \to 1} \frac{x^2 - 1}{x - 1} = \lim_{x \to 1} (x + 1) = 2$$

But $f(1)$ is undefined. Redefining $f(1) = 2$ removes the discontinuity.

**2. Jump Discontinuity:**
Both one-sided limits exist but are not equal: $\lim_{x \to a^-} f(x) \neq \lim_{x \to a^+} f(x)$.

*Example:* $f(x) = \begin{cases} 1, & x \ge 0 \\ 2, & x < 0 \end{cases}$ at $x = 0$.

$$\lim_{x \to 0^-} f(x) = 2, \quad \lim_{x \to 0^+} f(x) = 1$$

The "jump" from 2 to 1 cannot be removed.

**3. Infinite Discontinuity:**
At least one of the one-sided limits is $\pm\infty$.

*Example:* $f(x) = \dfrac{1}{x - 3}$ at $x = 3$.

$$\lim_{x \to 3} \frac{1}{x - 3} = \pm \infty \quad (\text{undefined})$$

**4. Oscillatory Discontinuity:**
The function oscillates infinitely often near $x = a$, so no limit exists.

*Example:* $f(x) = \sin\left(\dfrac{1}{x}\right)$ at $x = 0$. As $x \to 0$, the function oscillates between $-1$ and $+1$ without settling.

**[7 marks: 1.5 marks each for definition + 0.25 marks for example]**

---

#### Part (b) Solution

**Given:** $f$ and $g$ are continuous at $x = a$, i.e.,

$$\lim_{x \to a} f(x) = f(a) \quad \text{and} \quad \lim_{x \to a} g(x) = g(a)$$

**To Prove:** $f + g$, $f \cdot g$, and $f/g$ are continuous at $x = a$.

### Proof 1: Continuity of $f + g$

By the **algebra of limits**:

$$\lim_{x \to a} (f + g)(x) = \lim_{x \to a} [f(x) + g(x)]$$

Apply the sum rule of limits:

$$= \lim_{x \to a} f(x) + \lim_{x \to a} g(x)$$

Substitute the given continuity conditions:

$$= f(a) + g(a) = (f + g)(a)$$

Hence, $f + g$ is continuous at $x = a$. $\blacksquare$

### Proof 2: Continuity of $f \cdot g$

$$\lim_{x \to a} (f \cdot g)(x) = \lim_{x \to a} [f(x) \cdot g(x)]$$

Apply the product rule of limits:

$$= \lim_{x \to a} f(x) \cdot \lim_{x \to a} g(x) = f(a) \cdot g(a) = (f \cdot g)(a)$$

Hence, $f \cdot g$ is continuous at $x = a$. $\blacksquare$

### Proof 3: Continuity of $f/g$ (with $g(a) \neq 0$)

$$\lim_{x \to a} \left(\frac{f}{g}\right)(x) = \lim_{x \to a} \frac{f(x)}{g(x)}$$

Apply the quotient rule of limits (valid since $g(a) \neq 0$ implies $g(x) \neq 0$ in a neighborhood of $a$):

$$= \frac{\lim_{x \to a} f(x)}{\lim_{x \to a} g(x)} = \frac{f(a)}{g(a)} = \left(\frac{f}{g}\right)(a)$$

Hence, $f/g$ is continuous at $x = a$. $\blacksquare$

**[7 marks: 2 marks for setting up + 4 marks for using limit laws + 1 mark for concluding each proof]**

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU Module 2: Continuity**

- **Continuity at a point** requires **three conditions**: $f(a)$ defined, $\lim_{x \to a} f(x)$ exists, and $\lim_{x \to a} f(x) = f(a)$.
- **One-sided continuity:** Left-continuity + Right-continuity = Continuity at interior points.
- **Epsilon–Delta definition** is the **rigorous analytical form**; use it for proofs of continuity of simple linear/polynomial functions.
- **Discontinuities are of 4 types:** Removable, Jump, Infinite, Oscillatory. Each has a distinctive fingerprint in the limit behavior.
- **Polynomials, $\sin x$, $\cos x$, $e^x$, $\ln x$** are continuous on their entire domain (memorize this for KTU).
- **Algebraic closure:** Sum, difference, product, quotient (with non-zero denominator), and composition of continuous functions are continuous.
- **IVT:** A continuous function on $[a,b]$ takes **every value** between $f(a)$ and $f(b)$ — invaluable for **proving existence of roots**.
- **EVT:** A continuous function on a **closed bounded interval** attains its **maximum and minimum** — foundational for optimization in engineering.
- **KTU marking tip:** Always enumerate the three conditions and show $\frac{0}{0}$ resolution explicitly in $\lim$ calculations.
- **Common KTU trap:** A function may be continuous everywhere except at isolated points (e.g., $1/x$ at $x = 0$). Always test the **specific point asked**.
- **Engineering link:** Continuous transfer functions = stable systems. Discontinuities = switching events, fault conditions, or signal clipping.
- **Graphical signature:** Continuous curves are drawn **without lifting the pen**; any break, hole, or vertical asymptote indicates discontinuity.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Derivative
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** GYMAT301 - MATHEMATICS FOR ELECTRICAL SCIENCE AND PHYSICAL SCIENCE – 3
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Derivative

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **GYMAT301 (Mathematics for Electrical and Physical Science – 3, KTU 2024 Scheme)**, the **Derivative** of a real-valued function $f: \mathbb{R} \to \mathbb{R}$ at a point $x = a$ is formally defined as the **two-sided limit** of the **difference quotient** as the increment $h \to 0$, provided that limit exists and is finite.

$$f'(a) = \lim_{h \to 0} \frac{f(a+h) - f(a)}{h}$$

Equivalently, using the increment $\Delta x \to 0$:

$$f'(a) = \lim_{\Delta x \to 0} \frac{f(a + \Delta x) - f(a)}{\Delta x} = \lim_{x \to a} \frac{f(x) - f(a)}{x - a}$$

The function $f$ is said to be **differentiable at $x = a$** if and only if this limit exists. If the function is differentiable at every point in an open interval, it is called **differentiable on that interval**. The derivative operator $\dfrac{d}{dx}$ is the foundation upon which all differential calculus, integral calculus, and the entire field of differential equations is built.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 2):** The derivative is treated as a *rate-of-change operator* with explicit engineering interpretation. Students must master the **first-principles definition**, all **standard differentiation rules** (Sum, Product, Quotient, Chain), and the derivatives of **algebraic, trigonometric, exponential, logarithmic, and inverse-trigonometric** functions.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Speedometer" Interpretation

Imagine you are driving a car and your dashboard shows a **speedometer reading of 60 km/h**. That number is **not** the average speed over the entire trip — it is the **instantaneous rate** at which your distance from the starting point is changing **at this exact second**.

The derivative is the *mathematical speedometer* of any function. If $s(t)$ is the position of an object at time $t$, then $s'(t) = \dfrac{ds}{dt}$ gives the **instantaneous velocity** at that moment. In the same way, for an electrical engineering context, if $q(t)$ is the electric charge that has passed through a wire, then $i(t) = \dfrac{dq}{dt}$ gives the **instantaneous current** — a quantity universally measured in **Amperes (A)**.

Geometrically, the derivative $f'(a)$ equals the **slope of the tangent line** drawn to the curve $y = f(x)$ at the point $(a, f(a))$. The smaller the neighbourhood around $a$ you pick, the better the secant line approximates this tangent — which is the intuitive meaning of the limit.

> [!NOTE]
> **Differentiation vs. Differentiable:**
> - The *process* of computing a derivative is called **differentiation**.
> - A function is called **differentiable** at a point if its derivative **exists** at that point.
> - **Differentiability implies continuity**, but the converse is **not** true (e.g., $y = \vert x \vert$ is continuous but not differentiable at $x = 0$).

### 1.3 Key Notations (KTU High-Yield)

| Symbol | Read As | Origin |
|---|---|---|
| $f'(x)$ | f-prime of x | Lagrange notation |
| $\dfrac{dy}{dx}$ | dy by dx | Leibniz notation (favoured in KTU) |
| $\dfrac{d}{dx} f(x)$ | d by dx of f of x | Operator notation |
| $D_x f$ | D-sub-x of f | Euler notation |
| $\dot{y}$ | y-dot | Newton's notation (used for time-derivatives) |

### 1.4 Engineering Relevance Snapshot

The derivative is the single most-used mathematical tool across the **electrical and physical sciences**. The following applications are tested directly in the KTU 2024 syllabus:

- **Electrical Science:** Instantaneous current $i = \dfrac{dq}{dt}$ (Amperes), induced EMF $\varepsilon = -N\dfrac{d\Phi}{dt}$ (Volts), reactance of an inductor $V_L = L\dfrac{di}{dt}$.
- **Physical Science:** Instantaneous velocity $v = \dfrac{ds}{dt}$, acceleration $a = \dfrac{dv}{dt} = \dfrac{d^2 s}{dt^2}$, and the rate of radioactive decay $\dfrac{dN}{dt} = -\lambda N$.
- **Signal Processing:** Slope of any signal waveform $s(t)$ at time $t_0$ is $s'(t_0)$.

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Logic of the Limit Definition

Computing a derivative "from first principles" is a four-step disciplined process that KTU examiners reward consistently in Part A:

1. **Form the difference quotient:** $\dfrac{f(x+h) - f(x)}{h}$.
2. **Simplify the numerator:** Expand, factorise, or apply trigonometric identities.
3. **Cancel the common factor of $h$** (when possible) to remove the $\dfrac{0}{0}$ indeterminate form.
4. **Apply the limit** $h \to 0$ to the simplified expression.

The logical "Why" behind each step is: the difference quotient is a **secant slope**; the limit turns the secant into a **tangent slope** — the exact instantaneous rate.

### 2.2 Differentiation Rules — The Engineer's Toolkit

- **Constant Rule:** $\dfrac{d}{dx}(c) = 0$ — a constant function has zero rate of change. *"Why?":* Its graph is a horizontal line, whose tangent slope is zero.
- **Identity Rule:** $\dfrac{d}{dx}(x) = 1$ — the function $y = x$ is the reference 45° line.
- **Sum / Difference Rule:** $\dfrac{d}{dx}(u \pm v) = \dfrac{du}{dx} \pm \dfrac{dv}{dx}$ — derivatives distribute over addition.
- **Constant Multiple Rule:** $\dfrac{d}{dx}(c \cdot u) = c \cdot \dfrac{du}{dx}$.
- **Product Rule:** $\dfrac{d}{dx}(u \cdot v) = u \cdot \dfrac{dv}{dx} + v \cdot \dfrac{du}{dx}$ — *"The derivative of a product is the first times the derivative of the second plus the second times the derivative of the first."*
- **Quotient Rule:** $\dfrac{d}{dx}\!\left(\dfrac{u}{v}\right) = \dfrac{v \cdot \dfrac{du}{dx} - u \cdot \dfrac{dv}{dx}}{v^2}$ — *"Low d-high minus high d-low, over the square of the low."*
- **Chain Rule:** If $y = f(g(x))$, then $\dfrac{dy}{dx} = f'(g(x)) \cdot g'(x)$. The "outer derivative times the inner derivative."

### 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet — Standard Derivatives

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL FORMATTING NOTE:** Vertical bars denoting absolute value are written as $\vert \cdot \vert$ to avoid breaking the markdown table delimiter $\vert$.

| Function $f(x)$ | Derivative $f'(x)$ | Domain / Condition |
|---|---|---|
| $x^{n}$ | $n \cdot x^{n-1}$ | $n \in \mathbb{R}$ |
| $c$ (constant) | $0$ | $c \in \mathbb{R}$ |
| $\sin x$ | $\cos x$ | $x \in \mathbb{R}$ |
| $\cos x$ | $-\sin x$ | $x \in \mathbb{R}$ |
| $\tan x$ | $\sec^{2} x$ | $x \neq \frac{\pi}{2} + n\pi$ |
| $\sec x$ | $\sec x \tan x$ | $x \neq \frac{\pi}{2} + n\pi$ |
| $\csc x$ | $-\csc x \cot x$ | $x \neq n\pi$ |
| $\cot x$ | $-\csc^{2} x$ | $x \neq n\pi$ |
| $e^{x}$ | $e^{x}$ | $x \in \mathbb{R}$ |
| $a^{x}$ | $a^{x} \cdot \ln a$ | $a > 0, \ a \neq 1$ |
| $\ln \vert x \vert$ | $\dfrac{1}{x}$ | $x \neq 0$ |
| $\log_{a} \vert x \vert$ | $\dfrac{1}{x \ln a}$ | $a > 0, \ a \neq 1$ |
| $\sin^{-1} x$ | $\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{1-x^{2}}}$ | $\vert x \vert < 1$ |
| $\cos^{-1} x$ | $\dfrac{-1}{\sqrt{1-x^{2}}}$ | $\vert x \vert < 1$ |
| $\tan^{-1} x$ | $\dfrac{1}{1+x^{2}}$ | $x \in \mathbb{R}$ |

### 2.4 Higher-Order Derivatives

If $y = f(x)$ is differentiable, its derivative $f'(x)$ is itself a function and may be differentiated again:

- $f''(x) = \dfrac{d^{2}y}{dx^{2}}$ — the **second derivative** (acceleration in physics, concavity in geometry).
- $f'''(x) = \dfrac{d^{3}y}{dx^{3}}$ — the **third derivative** (rate of change of acceleration, "jerk").
- $f^{(n)}(x) = \dfrac{d^{n}y}{dx^{n}}$ — the **$n$-th derivative** in general.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Engineering Field | Derivative Application |
|---|---|
| **Circuit Analysis** | $V_L = L\dfrac{di}{dt}$ — Voltage across an inductor |
| **Electromagnetics** | Faraday's Law: $\varepsilon = -\dfrac{d\Phi_B}{dt}$ — induced EMF in Volts |
| **Power Systems** | Instantaneous power $p(t) = v(t)\,i(t)$; $p'(t)$ gives the rate of power change |
| **Control Systems** | Transfer function $H(s) = \mathcal{L}\{h'(t)\}$ links derivatives to system response |
| **Mechanical Vibration** | Newton's 2nd law: $F = m \cdot a = m \cdot \dfrac{d^{2}x}{dt^{2}}$ |
| **Signal Processing** | Edge detection in images uses the **gradient** $\nabla f = \left(\dfrac{\partial f}{\partial x}, \dfrac{\partial f}{\partial y}\right)$ |
| **Communication Systems** | FM modulation: instantaneous frequency $f_i(t) = f_c + \dfrac{1}{2\pi}\dfrac{d\phi}{dt}$ |

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> Every derivation below is **exhaustively worked out**. No steps are skipped. The aligned environment is used to maintain mathematical readability.

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Power Rule from First Principles

**Goal:** Prove that $\dfrac{d}{dx}(x^{n}) = n \cdot x^{n-1}$ for $n \in \mathbb{N}$ using the limit definition.

**Step 1 — Form the difference quotient:**

$$f'(x) = \lim_{h \to 0} \frac{f(x+h) - f(x)}{h} = \lim_{h \to 0} \frac{(x+h)^{n} - x^{n}}{h}$$

**Step 2 — Apply the Binomial Theorem** to expand $(x+h)^{n}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
(x+h)^{n} &= \sum_{k=0}^{n} \binom{n}{k} x^{n-k} h^{k} \\
&= x^{n} + n x^{n-1} h + \binom{n}{2} x^{n-2} h^{2} + \cdots + h^{n}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute back into the numerator** and cancel the $x^{n}$ terms:

$$
\begin{aligned}
(x+h)^{n} - x^{n} &= \left[x^{n} + n x^{n-1} h + \binom{n}{2} x^{n-2} h^{2} + \cdots + h^{n}\right] - x^{n} \\
&= n x^{n-1} h + \binom{n}{2} x^{n-2} h^{2} + \cdots + h^{n}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Factor out the common $h$:**

$$
(x+h)^{n} - x^{n} = h \left[ n x^{n-1} + \binom{n}{2} x^{n-2} h + \cdots + h^{n-1} \right]
$$

**Step 5 — Cancel the $h$** in the difference quotient:

$$
\frac{(x+h)^{n} - x^{n}}{h} = n x^{n-1} + \binom{n}{2} x^{n-2} h + \binom{n}{3} x^{n-3} h^{2} + \cdots + h^{n-1}
$$

**Step 6 — Apply the limit** $h \to 0$. Every term containing $h$ vanishes:

$$
\begin{aligned}
f'(x) &= \lim_{h \to 0} \left[ n x^{n-1} + \binom{n}{2} x^{n-2} h + \cdots + h^{n-1} \right] \\
&= n x^{n-1} + 0 + 0 + \cdots + 0 \\
&= n \cdot x^{n-1}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$\boxed{\dfrac{d}{dx}(x^{n}) = n \cdot x^{n-1}} \qquad \blacksquare$$

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Derivative of $\sin x$ from First Principles

**Goal:** Prove that $\dfrac{d}{dx}(\sin x) = \cos x$.

**Step 1 — Form the difference quotient:**

$$f'(x) = \lim_{h \to 0} \frac{\sin(x+h) - \sin x}{h}$$

**Step 2 — Apply the sum formula** $\sin(A+B) = \sin A \cos B + \cos A \sin B$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sin(x+h) &= \sin x \cos h + \cos x \sin h \\
\sin(x+h) - \sin x &= \sin x \cos h + \cos x \sin h - \sin x \\
&= \sin x (\cos h - 1) + \cos x \sin h
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Split into two limits:**

$$
f'(x) = \lim_{h \to 0} \left[ \sin x \cdot \frac{\cos h - 1}{h} + \cos x \cdot \frac{\sin h}{h} \right]
$$

**Step 4 — Use the two fundamental trigonometric limits:**

$$
\lim_{h \to 0} \frac{\sin h}{h} = 1 \qquad \text{and} \qquad \lim_{h \to 0} \frac{\cos h - 1}{h} = 0
$$

**Step 5 — Apply them:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
f'(x) &= \sin x \cdot \lim_{h \to 0} \frac{\cos h - 1}{h} + \cos x \cdot \lim_{h \to 0} \frac{\sin h}{h} \\
&= \sin x \cdot (0) + \cos x \cdot (1) \\
&= \cos x
\end{aligned}
$$

$$\boxed{\dfrac{d}{dx}(\sin x) = \cos x} \qquad \blacksquare$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Justification of** $\lim_{h \to 0} \dfrac{\cos h - 1}{h} = 0$: Multiply numerator and denominator by $(\cos h + 1)$ to get $\dfrac{\cos^{2} h - 1}{h(\cos h + 1)} = \dfrac{-\sin^{2} h}{h(\cos h + 1)} = -\dfrac{\sin h}{h} \cdot \dfrac{\sin h}{\cos h + 1} \to -(1)(0) = 0$.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Derivative of $e^{x}$ from First Principles

**Goal:** Prove that $\dfrac{d}{dx}(e^{x}) = e^{x}$ using the limit definition.

**Step 1 — Form the difference quotient:**

$$f'(x) = \lim_{h \to 0} \frac{e^{x+h} - e^{x}}{h}$$

**Step 2 — Use the law of exponents** $e^{x+h} = e^{x} \cdot e^{h}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{e^{x+h} - e^{x}}{h} &= \frac{e^{x} \cdot e^{h} - e^{x}}{h} \\
&= e^{x} \cdot \frac{e^{h} - 1}{h}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Factor out** $e^{x}$ (which is independent of $h$):

$$f'(x) = e^{x} \cdot \lim_{h \to 0} \frac{e^{h} - 1}{h}$$

**Step 4 — Recognize the fundamental exponential limit:**

$$\lim_{h \to 0} \frac{e^{h} - 1}{h} = 1$$

**Step 5 — Conclude:**

$$f'(x) = e^{x} \cdot 1 = e^{x}$$

$$\boxed{\dfrac{d}{dx}(e^{x}) = e^{x}} \qquad \blacksquare$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** The fact that $e^{x}$ is its own derivative is **the reason** the natural exponential appears in *every* natural-growth and natural-decay model — capacitor discharge $V(t) = V_{0} e^{-t/RC}$, RL circuit current $i(t) = \dfrac{V}{R}\left(1 - e^{-Rt/L}\right)$, and RC charging $q(t) = q_{0}(1 - e^{-t/RC})$.

---

### 3.4 Worked Example — Chain + Product Rule

**Problem:** Find $\dfrac{dy}{dx}$ for $y = e^{3x} \cdot \sin(2x)$.

**Step 1 — Identify the structure:** A product $u \cdot v$ where $u = e^{3x}$ and $v = \sin(2x)$, each requiring the **chain rule**.

**Step 2 — Differentiate each part:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{du}{dx} &= \frac{d}{dx}\left(e^{3x}\right) = e^{3x} \cdot \frac{d}{dx}(3x) = 3e^{3x} \\
\frac{dv}{dx} &= \frac{d}{dx}\left(\sin(2x)\right) = \cos(2x) \cdot \frac{d}{dx}(2x) = 2\cos(2x)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply the Product Rule** $\dfrac{dy}{dx} = u \dfrac{dv}{dx} + v \dfrac{du}{dx}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{dy}{dx} &= e^{3x} \cdot 2\cos(2x) + \sin(2x) \cdot 3e^{3x} \\
&= 2e^{3x}\cos(2x) + 3e^{3x}\sin(2x) \\
&= e^{3x}\bigl[2\cos(2x) + 3\sin(2x)\bigr]
\end{aligned}
$$

$$\boxed{\dfrac{dy}{dx} = e^{3x}\bigl[2\cos(2x) + 3\sin(2x)\bigr]}$$

---

### 3.5 Worked Example — Implicit Differentiation

**Problem:** Find $\dfrac{dy}{dx}$ for the curve $x^{2} + y^{2} = 25$.

**Step 1 — Differentiate both sides with respect to $x$** (treating $y$ as a function of $x$):

$$\frac{d}{dx}(x^{2}) + \frac{d}{dx}(y^{2}) = \frac{d}{dx}(25)$$

**Step 2 — Apply the chain rule** to $y^{2}$:

$$2x + 2y \cdot \frac{dy}{dx} = 0$$

**Step 3 — Solve for $\dfrac{dy}{dx}$:**

$$2y \cdot \frac{dy}{dx} = -2x \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \frac{dy}{dx} = -\frac{x}{y}$$

$$\boxed{\dfrac{dy}{dx} = -\dfrac{x}{y}}$$

This is the slope of the tangent to the circle at the point $(x, y)$ — and the result makes geometric sense, as it is the negative reciprocal of the radius slope $y/x$.

---

### 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Rule Selection Decision Table

| Form of $y$ | Primary Rule | Sub-rule Required |
|---|---|---|
| $y = (3x+1)^{5}$ | Chain | Power |
| $y = x^{2} \sin x$ | Product | Trigonometric |
| $y = \dfrac{\ln x}{x^{2}}$ | Quotient | Logarithmic |
| $y = x^{x}$ | Logarithmic Differentiation | Exponential form |
| $x^{2} + y^{2} = a^{2}$ | Implicit | Chain on $y^{2}$ |
| $x = a\cos t, \ y = a\sin t$ | Parametric | $\dfrac{dy}{dx} = \dfrac{dy/dt}{dx/dt}$ |
| $y = e^{\sin x}$ | Chain | Exponential + Trig |

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Conceptual Hierarchy of Differentiation

The following block-level flowchart maps the logical structure of the differentiation decision process as taught in Module 2 of GYMAT301.

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[START: Given Function y equals f of x] --> B{Is f given in explicit form y equals f of x?}
    B -- YES --> C{Is f a composite function f of g of x?}
    B -- NO --> D{Is f defined implicitly F of x comma y equals 0?}
    C -- YES --> E[Apply CHAIN RULE outer derivative times inner derivative]
    C -- NO --> F{Is f a product or quotient of two simpler functions?}
    F -- PRODUCT --> G[Apply PRODUCT RULE u dv by dx plus v du by dx]
    F -- QUOTIENT --> H[Apply QUOTIENT RULE v du by dx minus u dv by dx over v squared]
    F -- NEITHER --> I[Use STANDARD DERIVATIVE TABLE directly]
    D -- YES --> J[Differentiate both sides with respect to x then isolate dy by dx]
    E --> K[SIMPLIFY the final expression]
    G --> K
    H --> K
    I --> K
    J --> K
    K --> L[OUTPUT: derivative dy by dx]
```

### 4.2 Function Classification Tree for Standard Derivatives

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Function Type] --> B[Algebraic]
    A --> C[Trigonometric]
    A --> D[Exponential]
    A --> E[Logarithmic]
    A --> F[Inverse Trigonometric]
    B --> B1[Power Rule: d by dx of x to the n equals n x to the n minus 1]
    C --> C1[Sin x goes to Cos x]
    C --> C2[Cos x goes to minus Sin x]
    C --> C3[Tan x goes to Sec squared x]
    D --> D1[e to the x is its own derivative]
    D --> D2[a to the x gives a to the x times ln a]
    E --> E1[ln of x gives 1 over x]
    E --> E2[log base a of x gives 1 over x times ln a]
    F --> F1[Sin inverse x gives 1 over root 1 minus x squared]
    F --> F2[Cos inverse x gives minus 1 over root 1 minus x squared]
    F --> F3[Tan inverse x gives 1 over 1 plus x squared]
```

### 4.3 Block-Level Engineering Application Flow

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Physical Quantity q of t] --> B[Differentiation Operator d by dt]
    B --> C[Instantaneous Current i of t]
    C --> D[Circuit Equation: V equals L di by dt]
    D --> E[Solved Response: i of t equals V over R times 1 minus e to the minus R t over L]
    E --> F[Time Constant tau equals L over R in seconds]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** This block-level architecture shows how a derivative (the $\frac{d}{dt}$ operator) bridges a *physical observable* (charge) to an *engineered response* (inductor current), and how integration of that derivative yields the closed-form solution with the **time constant $\tau = L/R$** measured in **seconds (s)**.

### 4.4 Geometric Meaning of the Derivative

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Curve y equals f of x] --> B[Pick a point P with x equals a]
    B --> C[Take a second point Q with x equals a plus h]
    C --> D[Form secant line PQ with slope m secant]
    D --> E[Move Q towards P by letting h approach 0]
    E --> F[Secant line becomes TANGENT LINE at P]
    F --> G[Slope of tangent equals f prime of a equals dy by dx at x equals a]
```
